5 min read
Just now

--

Learn how webhooks power instant notifications, automate workflows, and securely connect modern applications.

Introduction
Have you ever wondered how your favorite apps seem to know exactly when something happens?

• You receive an email the moment a payment succeeds.

• GitHub automatically starts a CI/CD pipeline after you push code.

• Slack sends a notification when someone creates a support ticket.

• Shopify updates inventory immediately after an order is placed.
These actions happen within seconds — without anyone clicking a refresh button. Behind the scenes, one simple technology makes all of this possible:
Webhooks :
Although they’re one of the most widely used integration technologies, webhooks are often misunderstood by new developers. In this article, we’ll learn what webhooks are, why they exist, how they work, where they’re used, and the basic security practices you should always follow.
Why Were Webhooks Created?
Imagine you’re building an online store and need to know when a customer’s payment has been completed. One option is to repeatedly ask the payment provider:
“Has the payment completed?”
If the answer is No, your application waits a few seconds and asks again. This process is called polling. Most of these requests return exactly the same answer:
“Nothing has changed.”
As your application grows, these unnecessary requests waste bandwidth, server resources, and processing power. Developers needed a better solution. Instead of constantly asking for updates, why not let the service notify your application only when something important happens? That’s exactly why webhooks were introduced.
What Is a Webhook?
A webhook is an HTTP callback that allows one application to automatically notify another application whenever a specific event occurs.
Rather than continuously requesting updates, your application provides a URL (called a webhook endpoint) where it can receive notifications.
When an event happens, the service sends an HTTP POST request containing information about that event. In simple terms:
A webhook is an automatic message sent from one application to another when something important happens.
Polling vs Webhooks
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Understanding the difference between polling and webhooks is essential.
Polling : Think of it like ordering food. You keep calling the restaurant every five minutes:
“Is my order ready yet?”
Webhook: The restaurant simply calls you once:
“Your order is ready.”
Much simpler. Much faster. Much more efficient.
How Do Webhooks Work?
A webhook usually follows four simple steps.
Step 1 — Register a Webhook URL
Your application tells the service where notifications should be sent. Example:
https://example.com/webhooks/paymentStep 2 — An Event Occurs
Something important happens. Examples include:

• Payment completed

• User registered

• GitHub repository updated

• Order shipped

• Invoice paid
Step 3 — The Service Sends a Request
The provider sends an HTTP POST request to your webhook endpoint. Example payload:
{
"event": "payment.success",
"customer": "John",
"amount": 99.99,
"currency": "USD"
}Step 4 — Your Application Processes the Event
Your application receives the request, validates it, performs the required work, and returns an HTTP 200 OK response.
A simplified flow looks like this:
Customer Makes Payment
│
▼
Payment Provider
│
Payment Successful
│
▼
HTTP POST Request
│
▼
Your Webhook Endpoint
│
▼
Validate Request
│
▼
Process Business Logic
│
▼
Return HTTP 200 OKA Real-World Example
Imagine you’re building an e-commerce website using Stripe for payments. When a customer completes payment:

• Stripe detects the successful payment.

• Stripe sends a webhook to your application.

• Your application verifies the request.

• The order is marked as paid.

• Inventory is updated.

• A confirmation email is sent.

• An invoice is generated.
Everything happens automatically. No polling. No manual intervention.
Where Are Webhooks Used?
Webhooks are everywhere. Some common use cases include:

• Payment notifications

• CI/CD pipelines

• Git repository events

• Chat applications

• Order processing

• Customer relationship management (CRM)

• Email automation

• Calendar notifications

• IoT devices

• Monitoring and alerting systems
Popular platforms that support webhooks include GitHub, Stripe, Shopify, Slack, Discord, Twilio, Notion, GitLab, and PayPal.
Basic Webhook Security
Because webhook endpoints are exposed to the internet, they should never blindly trust incoming requests. Here are the most important security practices every developer should follow.
1. Always Use HTTPS
Webhook data often contains sensitive business information. Using HTTPS encrypts communication between the sender and your server, protecting it from interception.
2. Verify the Signature
Many providers include a cryptographic signature with every webhook request. Your application should calculate its own signature using the shared secret and compare it with the received signature. If they don’t match, reject the request immediately. This ensures the request actually came from the trusted provider.
3. Validate Timestamps
Attackers may try to resend an old webhook request. By checking the timestamp and rejecting requests older than a few minutes, you can reduce the risk of replay attacks.
4. Validate the Payload
Never assume incoming JSON is valid.
Always verify:

• Required fields exist.

• Data types are correct.

• Values are within expected ranges.
Input validation helps prevent unexpected behavior and improves application reliability.
5. Prevent Duplicate Processing
Webhook providers may retry delivery if they don’t receive a successful response. This means the same event can arrive more than once. Store the event ID and make sure each event is processed only once. This concept is called idempotency. Without it, a payment might accidentally be processed twice.
6. Respond Quickly
Don’t perform long-running work before responding.
Instead:

• Validate the request.

• Store or queue the event.

• Return 200 OK immediately.

• Process the event in the background.
This improves reliability and reduces unnecessary retries.
Advantages of Webhooks
Webhooks provide several important benefits:

• Real-time notifications

• Reduced network traffic

• Lower latency

• Better scalability

• Easier automation

• Faster system integration

• Efficient event-driven communication
Challenges
Like any technology, webhooks have limitations.
Some common challenges include:

• Internet connectivity issues

• Duplicate deliveries

• Endpoint downtime

• Retry handling

• Security validation

• Event ordering
Fortunately, these challenges can be addressed with good engineering practices.
Best Practices
Before deploying webhook endpoints, make sure you:

• Use HTTPS.

• Verify request signatures.

• Validate timestamps.

• Validate payloads.

• Process events idempotently.

• Respond quickly with HTTP 200 OK.

• Log webhook deliveries.

• Monitor failures and retries.

• Process long-running tasks asynchronously.
Following these practices will make your webhook integrations far more reliable and secure.
Conclusion
Webhooks are one of the simplest yet most powerful technologies behind modern software integrations.
Instead of constantly asking whether something has changed, applications simply wait to be notified when an event occurs. This event-driven approach reduces unnecessary network traffic, improves performance, and enables real-time automation across thousands of services.
Whether you’re building payment systems, automating workflows, integrating third-party APIs, or creating cloud-native applications, understanding webhooks is an essential skill for every developer.
The next time you receive an instant payment confirmation, see a deployment start automatically, or watch an order status update in real time, there’s a good chance a webhook is working quietly behind the scenes.
Remember: APIs let applications ask questions. Webhooks let applications automatically deliver answers when something important happens.
